quote: Hey, David. This is Holland with Ion. So I just got a request that you were looking into solar for your home. How are you doing today?
stewart_read: Holland skips the legitimacy-establishing intro entirely. Neither the tesla variant nor the standard variant is executed — there is no company framing, no reference to how Ion got David's information, and no credibility anchor in the first 30 seconds. The call opens with a vague 'I just got a request' that does not tell David who Ion is, why they are calling, or why he should trust them. For a cooperative customer this did not trigger resistance, but the codex is explicit: legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may have judged that David's cooperative tone made a full intro unnecessary. Rejected — the intro's function is legitimacy, not rapport, and it is required regardless of customer warmth.
coaching_implication: Pull this opening with Holland and ask: 'What does the customer know about Ion after your first sentence?' Walk through intros.standard — the form-fill reference and company name are the two legitimacy anchors. On a Tesla-sourced lead, intros.tesla does the same work. Neither was used here. This is a habit gap, not a one-call anomaly — check Holland's other calls for the same pattern.

quote: And then I also was kinda curious what got you guys looking into solar?
stewart_read: David's answer at 01:22–01:45 is the richest moment in the call. He volunteers two distinct motivations — environmental values ('cleaner and greener') and bill pain ('going up, up, up, up') — with genuine elaboration. This is the emotional anchor the entire pitch should be built around. Holland's response at 01:46 ('the electric bills have been going crazy lately, so I do not blame you guys') acknowledges the bill pain but does not anchor it, does not flip it, and does not tie it forward to the appointment. The environmental motivation is not acknowledged at all. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been moving efficiently through verify items and planned to return to motivation depth later. Rejected — there is no later return to this material in the transcript.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage missed moment. Ask Holland: 'David told you exactly why he wants solar — what did you do with that?' Walk through coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion: the flip on bill pain is 'that's the number Dominion is taking from you every month — the design is built to change that.' The environmental motivation is a secondary anchor: 'You want to go cleaner AND stop paying Dominion more every year — that's exactly what the design addresses.' Both were available; neither was used.

quote: K. And then yeah. Right now so we have a pretty good program going on. So what it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar. As long as you have a credit score above 6 70, then you'll qualify. Is Is that the case for you guys?
stewart_read: Holland transitions directly from bill confirmation ($250/month) to the credit qualifier without anchoring the bill emotionally. The bill is captured as data and dropped. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the capture-and-drop pattern is the universal failure mode across the corpus. The $250 figure David provided is the emotional center of the pitch — it represents what David is paying Dominion every month — and it passes without being named as the problem the design is solving.
coaching_implication: Ask Holland: 'After David told you his bill was around $250 a month, what did you say next?' The suggested rephrase is: 'So you're sending Dominion $250 every month — that's the number the design is built around.' This names the pain without implying a specific offset. The goal is to make David feel the bill before moving to credit qualification, not to quote savings. See coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring: capture + flip, not capture + move on.

quote: Last time I checked, it was right around there. It was it was called, I think, the FICO that's FICO usually. Right? That's kind of the Yeah. The short they took. It was late. They don't call it very good. Can't remember if it's, like, 670 or 675, something like that.
stewart_read: David's self-reported credit score is explicitly uncertain — he cannot confirm whether it is 670 or 675, and he is recalling from memory. Holland qualifies him at 03:07 ('you guys would qualify, which is great news') without any hedge. Per Spencer's operator note in qualifiers.credit_qualifier, stated credit is unreliable — customers see Vantage scores, not FICO; three bureaus often differ by 30–50 points. Holland is making a qualification decision on a number David himself cannot confirm. If the actual pulled score comes in below 670, this qualification is a false positive that wastes the closer's time and potentially the setter's sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may be applying the 'let the closer's pull decide' philosophy and treating the stated number as sufficient to proceed. Possible — but the codex flags this as an unresolved policy question (qualifiers.credit_qualifier.policy_question_for_kenny), and Holland's phrasing ('you guys would qualify') presents the outcome as certain rather than conditional.
coaching_implication: This is a policy question before it is a coaching question. Bring to Kenny: is the setter's job to qualify on stated credit or to pass through to the closer's pull? Until that is resolved, coach Holland to hedge: 'Sounds like you're in the right range — the specialist will run a soft pull to confirm when you meet.' This keeps the appointment alive without overclaiming qualification on an uncertain number.

quote: Yeah. I'm not I'm not seeing it at the moment, so it's not something I can probably produce right this morning. I'm also about to head out on a three day trip in about ten minutes.
stewart_read: David cannot locate the paper bill and is leaving in ten minutes. Holland's response is to send a text and ask David to photo the bill when he has a minute — a passive deferral that converts a live, qualified, cooperative customer into a cold follow-up. The codex's shopping_customer_protocol is directly applicable here: David is on his phone, has a Dominion account, and has ten minutes. The actionable alternative was to walk David to the Dominion app or website to pull kWh usage live before he left. Holland did not attempt this path.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Holland failed to attempt the utility app path and defaulted to passive follow-up. Strategic read: Holland judged that David's ten-minute window was too tight for a live app walk-through and preserved goodwill by not pressuring him. The reactive read is more supported — David was cooperative and the app path takes two to three minutes with a guided walk-through. The strategic read would require evidence that Holland assessed the time constraint and made a deliberate choice; no such evidence exists in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Ask Holland: 'David was on his phone with a Dominion account and ten minutes before he had to leave — what else could you have tried?' Walk through bill_collection.alternative_paths.shopping_customer_protocol: 'Do you have the Dominion app on your phone? Let me walk you through pulling your kWh usage right now — takes about two minutes.' This is the path that keeps the call alive. The paper bill was unavailable; the app was not attempted.

quote: I believe it's probably my wife as well.
stewart_read: Holland confirms the wife is on the title at 01:09 and acknowledges it ('Your wife as well? Okay. Yeah.') but never returns to spouse inclusion for the appointment. The call ends with a Friday callback framed around Holland following up with David alone. Per protocols.spouse_decision, a co-title holder must be included in the appointment — not as a courtesy but as a structural requirement for the close. The wife's absence from the follow-up plan is a protocol gap that makes any eventual appointment structurally vulnerable.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may have planned to address spouse inclusion during button-up, which never occurred because no appointment was booked. Partially valid — but the Friday callback is the de facto appointment, and the wife was not included in that plan either.
coaching_implication: Ask Holland: 'You confirmed the wife is on the title — where does she appear in your follow-up plan?' The correct move at 04:46 was: 'When I call Friday, will your wife be available to join? Since she's on the title, we want to make sure she's part of the conversation.' This is not a persuasion move — it is a protocol. See protocols.spouse_decision: include the co-title holder in the appointment, not as an afterthought.

quote: Thursday evening? K. So maybe I'll if I don't hear from you, maybe I'll just follow-up on Friday. Would that work?
stewart_read: The word 'maybe' and the conditional 'if I don't hear from you' frame this as a soft, rep-initiated follow-up rather than a locked commitment. No time is confirmed for Friday. No button-up sequence is executed — no one-hour block, no computer requirement, no 'is that fair' handshake. Per analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time is structurally fragile. David's own hedge at 05:08 ('I'll try to') confirms the outcome is not a commitment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may have judged that pressing for a locked time on a customer about to leave for three days would create friction and reduce the likelihood of the Friday call. Possible — but the codex is explicit that a locked time is the difference between a callback and a sit. The soft framing here is a habit pattern, not a deliberate strategic choice.
coaching_implication: Walk Holland through button_up protocol and ask: 'What would a locked Friday callback look like?' The suggested rephrase: 'Let's put Friday on the calendar right now — what time works for you and your wife? I'll send you a text with the details so you have it.' This converts a soft 'maybe Friday' into a locked time with both decision-makers confirmed. The button-up sequence exists precisely for this moment.

quote: Good. Well, I can keep the call super brief for you. So I just help get those estimates put together for the home. And I just have a few things that I need to confirm with you real quick.
stewart_read: Holland opens by preemptively minimizing the call's value — 'super brief,' 'just a few things,' 'real quick.' David has not expressed any time pressure at this point. This framing signals low confidence in the call's worth and trains the customer to expect nothing of substance. It is the opposite of curiosity creation: instead of making David want to stay on the line, Holland is apologizing for taking his time before he has asked for it back.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may be using a low-pressure open to reduce resistance on a cold-ish customer. Partially valid as a tactic — but David's tone at 00:10 was neutral, not resistant. The low-pressure frame was not earned by the customer's behavior; it was Holland's default posture.
coaching_implication: Ask Holland: 'What does David know about why this call is worth his time after your first two sentences?' The opening frame sets the call's ceiling. A suggested rephrase: 'I help homeowners in Williamsburg figure out what solar would actually look like for their specific home — I just need a few minutes to pull your info together.' This names the value before minimizing the time. See coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_features: the goal is to make the customer curious, not to apologize for calling.
